did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically Vision Transformer (ViT) and VMamba (a spatial state model), for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset. The models were pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned to classify skin lesions, with performance evaluated using accuracy and F1-score metrics.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing Vision Transformer (ViT) and VMamba models for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific sections for Background, Objective, Methods, Results (with Table 1 showing accuracy and F1-scores), and Conclusions, indicating it is an empirical study rather than a review.

title: TRANSFORMER VS. MAMBA AS SKIN CANCER CLASSIFIER: PRELIMINARY RESULTS
brief_summary: This study compared the performance of Vision Transformer (ViT) and VMamba architectures for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset. Both models were pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned to classify malignant and benign skin lesions, with evaluation metrics including overall accuracy and F1-scores for specific classes. The results indicated that VMamba achieved similar overall accuracy to ViT while demonstrating slightly superior performance on underrepresented classes such as Bowen’s disease and dermatofibroma. Both models showed high effectiveness in detecting melanoma, with F1-scores reaching 0.9. The authors conclude that VMamba is a viable and potentially more cost-effective alternative to ViT due to its linear scaling complexity. Further research is recommended to explore other Mamba variants and enhance performance on larger datasets.

lesion_types_studied.details: Grouped as a single underrepresented class in the classification task; evaluated using F1-score.
Identified as a rare/underrepresented class; evaluated using F1-score.
Identified as the most severe and dangerous type of skin cancer; evaluated using F1-score.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant, specific lesion classes)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories including Actinic Keratosis/Intraepithelial Carcinoma/Bowen's Disease (AKEIC), Dermatofibroma (Df), and Melanoma (Mel). Performance was evaluated using overall accuracy and F1-scores for these specific classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A well-known benchmark dataset consisting of 10,015 dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, including both malignant and benign cases. The images were cleaned and centered. Duplicates were eliminated based on lesion ID, and pixel intensities were normalized to the 0-1 range. Oversampling (rotations, flips, zooms) was applied to mitigate class imbalance, aiming for at least 7,000 samples per class.

use.training: The dataset's provided training data was split into training and validation sets using a consistent random seed. The dataset was also used for fine-tuning the pre-trained ViT and VMamba models.
use.validation: A portion of the original training data was used for validation to monitor loss and stop training when no improvements were observed for 3 consecutive epochs.
use.testing: The dataset's provided test data was used for final evaluation of accuracy and F1-scores.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale image dataset used for pre-training the base architectures of the Vision Transformer (ViT) and VMamba models before fine-tuning on the skin cancer task.

use.training: Used for pre-training the ViT and VMamba models via weights provided by other research teams.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper compares performance metrics (accuracy and F1-scores) descriptively without statistical significance tests.

HAM10000.notes: VMamba models showed slightly superior performance on underrepresented classes (Bowen’s disease/AKEIC and dermatofibroma) compared to ViT. Both models demonstrated high F1-scores for Melanoma (0.87-0.9). ViT_L_32 had the highest training accuracy (0.98) but lowest generalizability (Top-1 Acc 0.76). VMamba-T and VMamba-B achieved F1 scores of 0.6 and 0.65 respectively for AKEIC. For Dermatofibroma, VMamba-T (0.76) slightly outperformed ViT_L_16 (0.75). For Melanoma, VMamba-B and ViT_L_16 both achieved an F1 score of 0.9.

architecture_used.main_model: Vision Transformer (ViT) and VMamba (Visual State Space Model)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset using weights provided by other research teams (imagenet1_v1, imagenet1k_swag_linear_v1 for ViT; vssmtiny_dp02_ckpt_epoch_258, vssm_base_0229_ckpt_epoch_237 for VMamba).
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images were cleaned, centered, and normalized to 0-1 range).

architecture_details.VMamba_specifics: Utilizes a 2D selective scan mechanism involving unfolding image patches into sequences along rows and columns and scanning in four directions (top-left to bottom-right, bottom-right to top-left, top-right to bottom-left, bottom-left to top-right).
architecture_details.ViT_specifics: Based on the Vision Transformer architecture originally designed for NLP, adapted for computer vision.

training_details.epochs: Up to 20 epochs; on average approximately 15 epochs.
training_details.learning_rates: Tested from 10^-6 to 10^-8; only top learning rates presented.
training_details.early_stopping: Training stopped when validation loss showed no improvements for 3 consecutive epochs.
training_details.loss_function: Not reported (evaluation based on accuracy and F1-score).

classification_or_output_head.structure: Custom classifier comprising a sequence of layers: Flatten, Dropout, Norm, Linear, GELU, Dropout, Norm, Linear, Softmax.

preprocessing_techniques: Deduplication: Duplicate images eliminated based on lesion ID to ensure unique samples.
Normalization: Pixel intensities normalized to the 0–1 range to standardize input data.
Oversampling/Augmentation: Applied rotations, flips, and zooms to mitigate class imbalance and artificially enhance underrepresented classes.
Train-Validation Split: Training data divided into training and validation sets using a consistent random seed.
Uniformity enforcement: Ensured uniform use of augmented images and train-test-validation split across all experiments.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied rotations, flips, and zooms to artificially enhance underrepresented classes and mitigate class imbalance. The goal was to achieve at least 7,000 samples per class.

conclusions: Mamba is a viable alternative to Vision Transformer (ViT) for skin cancer classification due to its similar overall accuracy.
VMamba models demonstrated slightly superior performance on underrepresented classes, specifically Bowen’s disease (AKEIC) and dermatofibroma, compared to ViT.
Both ViT and VMamba models achieved high F1-scores (0.9) for melanoma detection, indicating high effectiveness in identifying this severe form of skin cancer.
The application of VMamba could potentially make skin cancer diagnosis cheaper and more accessible due to its linear scaling complexity, addressing the computational limitations of Transformers.
ViT_L_32 exhibited the lowest generalizability to new data among the tested models, with a top-1 accuracy of 0.76 despite high training accuracy.
Further research is needed to explore other variants of the Mamba model, enhance performance on larger datasets, and reconsider classification heads and learning strategies.
important_remarks: The study is labeled as 'preliminary results' and is a conference paper, indicating limited scope and maturity of the findings.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the HAM10000 dataset, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between ViT and VMamba, relying only on descriptive metric comparisons.
Exact computational resource ratios (e.g., FLOPs, inference time, memory usage) were not assessed, despite the claim that Mamba is more resource-efficient than Transformers.

The dataset was artificially balanced using oversampling (rotations, flips, zooms) to achieve at least 7,000 samples per class, which may not reflect real-world class distribution and could lead to over-optimistic performance estimates.

The custom classification head architecture (Flatten, Dropout, Norm, Linear, GELU, etc.) was not derived from a standardized design but was a result of experimental tuning, potentially introducing bias.
The study focuses on a limited set of lesion types (AKEIC, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma) and does not address the full spectrum of skin cancer classes present in HAM10000.
The authors acknowledge that further research is needed to explore other Mamba variants and fine-tune on larger datasets, suggesting current results are not definitive.
